Who's Who in Legendre Conjecture Research
Home Top 100 Location In Memoriam Genealogy Network Reading List Overlap About

Mathematical Genealogy

Close relations of the Top 100, by proximity score

Every PhD mathematician has an advisor, and every advisor has an advisor. The Mathematics Genealogy Project catalogs this lineage. We use it to surface close relations: researchers not in the Top 100 by publication count, but who sit in its immediate orbit through advisor-student relationships.

How the proximity score works

We build one graph from the advisor and student trees of all Top 100 researchers. A close relation is an outsider directly tied (as advisor or student) to at least two Top 100 researchers. Each gets a proximity score that sums, over those direct ties, 101 minus the connected researcher's rank. Being tied to the top of the list counts for much more than being tied to the bottom. Only direct ties count; people already in the directory and deceased mathematicians are excluded.

Level L1

ResearcherPhD universityPhD yearDirect Top 100 tiesProximity score

Notes

  • Jean-Marc Deshouillers (Universit&eacute; Pierre-et-Marie-Curie - Paris VI, 1972): A mathematician (PhD Universit&eacute; Pierre-et-Marie-Curie - Paris VI, 1972) directly tied to 4 Top 100 researchers through advisor-student relationships in the genealogy network.
  • Glyn Harman (University of London, 1982): A mathematician (PhD University of London, 1982) directly tied to 2 Top 100 researchers through advisor-student relationships in the genealogy network.
  • Chengdong Pan (潘承洞) (University, 1961): A mathematician (PhD University, 1961) directly tied to 2 Top 100 researchers through advisor-student relationships in the genealogy network.
  • Olli Järviniemi (University, 2023): A mathematician (PhD University, 2023) directly tied to 2 Top 100 researchers through advisor-student relationships in the genealogy network.
  • Jared Duker Lichtman (University of Oxford, 2023): A mathematician (PhD University of Oxford, 2023) directly tied to 2 Top 100 researchers through advisor-student relationships in the genealogy network.
  • Dorian Morris Goldfeld (University, 1969): A mathematician (PhD University, 1969) directly tied to 2 Top 100 researchers through advisor-student relationships in the genealogy network.
  • Douglas Robert Woodall (University of Nottingham, 1969): A mathematician (PhD University of Nottingham, 1969) directly tied to 2 Top 100 researchers through advisor-student relationships in the genealogy network.
  • Mattia Cafferata (Universit&agrave; degli Studi di Ferrara, 2019): A mathematician (PhD Universit&agrave; degli Studi di Ferrara, 2019) directly tied to 2 Top 100 researchers through advisor-student relationships in the genealogy network.
  • Peter Clive Sarnak (University, 1980): A mathematician (PhD University, 1980) directly tied to 2 Top 100 researchers through advisor-student relationships in the genealogy network.
  • Huafeng Liu (University, 2017): A mathematician (PhD University, 2017) directly tied to 2 Top 100 researchers through advisor-student relationships in the genealogy network.
  • Taiyu Li (University, 2012): A mathematician (PhD University, 2012) directly tied to 2 Top 100 researchers through advisor-student relationships in the genealogy network.
  • Stefan Neumann (Universit&auml;t Stuttgart, 2006): A mathematician (PhD Universit&auml;t Stuttgart, 2006) directly tied to 2 Top 100 researchers through advisor-student relationships in the genealogy network.
  • Olga Balkanova (Universit&agrave; degli Studi di Milano and Universit&eacute; Bordeau, 2015): A mathematician (PhD Universit&agrave; degli Studi di Milano and Universit&eacute; Bordeau, 2015) directly tied to 2 Top 100 researchers through advisor-student relationships in the genealogy network.
  • Katalin Gyarmati (University and Universit&eacute; Henri Poincar&eacute; Nancy 1, 2005): A mathematician (PhD University and Universit&eacute; Henri Poincar&eacute; Nancy 1, 2005) directly tied to 2 Top 100 researchers through advisor-student relationships in the genealogy network.
  • Francesco Monopoli (University, 2015): A mathematician (PhD University, 2015) directly tied to 2 Top 100 researchers through advisor-student relationships in the genealogy network.

Maintained by Steve Hubbard (admin@wwileg.org). Data sources: arXiv, OpenAlex, zbMATH Open, Mathematics Genealogy Project.

Last built June 2026.
